opening_excerpt:
Your introduction is always the title first. Your jaw recognizes the order: title, then person, then nothing.

You got the promotion and your internal valuation went up. The deal fell through and it went down. You are running a constant market valuation on yourself. Your worth fluctuates with the external signals. When things are winning, you feel valuable. When things stall, you feel diminished. Your sense of yourself has become a traded security. This is the exit multiple on yourself—the internal valuation that adjusts with market conditions, the sense of worth that is tethered to outcomes instead of being stable.

That's the exit multiple on yourself. It will come up throughout this book — not because it's the only thing happening, but because it tends to be underneath most of what we're going to look at.

You ask for help. Asking for help is admitting you can't do the project alone. {Lexington Avenue below is where the help arrives. Fluorescent station light streaks is support arriving. {the downtown Q train brings the help. Sarah helps. Sarah who is first. First helping fourth is hierarchy visible. The helping is kind. The kind is also confirmation. Confirmation that Sarah is better at this. Confirmation that Sarah is first because she's better. Confirmation that you're fourth because you're not as good. The help makes the project survivable. The help also makes the fourth visible.

Stay with the moment a second longer. The pattern usually shows itself before the explanation does.

Shame selects the worst available interpretation and presents it as the only interpretation. It curates a database of your exposures and deletes evidence of competence. The assessment always finds you lacking because the database only contains evidence of lacking.

The point is that the exit multiple on yourself treats every decision like a permanent threat.

There is a particular vulnerability in the sense of self-worth when it is used as a defense against shame. The person has built the confident exterior and the sense of achievement as a way of defending against shame. If I am accomplished and recognized, I do not have to feel the shame that I fear underneath. The exit multiple on yourself is the shame-defense through achievement. The achievement is being used to defend against the shame. I have felt this most acutely in the awareness that the drive to achieve might be partially about defending against shame. The sense that without the achievement, the shame would surface.
